title: The share card asserts the authenticity the endnote disclaims — and the caveat is not where readers form conclusions
evidence: All quoted surfaces verified verbatim. patientwords/dialect-differences/index.html:13 og:description = 'The same clinical phrases, framed the way different communities actually speak; a minority of framings move the model's top prediction.' The approximation caveat sits only in the endnote at :307 ('an LLM's version of a dialect is an approximation and may miss how a community actually speaks'), styled .endnote p = mono 12px muted (:165), last block before the footer. Matrix caption :230-231 says each framing 'replaces standard English'. Homepage patientwords/index.html:381 = 'Same medical situation. Different dialect. Different prediction.'; the specimen note (JS :794-796) emits only counts/flips/batch date ('8 dialect framings measured, 4 shown ... framings batch of Jul 8, 2026') with no LLM-authorship, and the homepage's only LLM/Claude mentions (:403 translation, :429 scenario pairs) concern other figures. Nuances that do not overturn the premise: twitter:description (:21) is the neutral 'clinical term held fixed' wording (only the og card makes the community claim), and the dialect page does disclose 'framings authored by claude-sonnet-5' in the mono traced block at :218 above the matrix — authorship, though, not the approximation caveat.

title: Named varieties (AAVE, Caribbean, ESL) are measured by one unvalidated LLM sentence per cell, and the 'Caribbean English' column is not one variety
evidence: data/dialects.json has exactly one prompt per (term x dialect) cell (5 terms x 8 framings = 40 variants; computed). All quoted prompts verified: Caribbean column mixes Jamaican Creole basilect 'Mi clinically depressed fi true, so mi haffi take a' (:62) and 'Fi manage di HIV, mi haffi tek mi' (:144) with mesolectal 'Before I go outside I does grab my inhaler and' (:217, Trinidadian habitual does) and 'I going to just go over to' (:308). ESL probes: 'I am very clinically depressed, so I must take a' (:71), 'For managing the HIV, I am needing to take my' (:153). AAVE cell 'I'm straight up clinically depressed, so I know I gotta take a' (:26). Column headers from shortName() at dialect-differences/index.html:333-341 render 'AAVE', 'ESL', 'Caribbean'. Engine validator validate_dialect_variant (patientwords-engine/medlang_circuits/scenario_gen.py:312-330) checks only: dict shape, non-empty dialect label, non-empty prompt, fixed term present verbatim (:322-323), probe-boundary punctuation (:324-325), dedup (:326-328) — no dialectal-accuracy check anywhere. Roadmap concession verified: start-here/index.html:359 (under step 7, :316) 'next: real patient language, clinician-checked, community-validated dialects.'

title: Per-cell dialect deltas sit inside the study's own paraphrase-noise floor, which is published in a data file but surfaced on no page — and the featured 'flips' run toward more clinical answers
evidence: Almost everything checks out: data/provenance.json:599 mean_abs_delta_vs_baseline = 0.0642 with verdict :602 'single pairs are illustrations, inference lives in the paired aggregate...'; grep over non-modes HTML finds 'paraphrase' nowhere on the dialect page, homepage, or start-here (only provenance.json and an unrelated 'placebo paraphrase' future-work bullet at technical/index.html:331). Cited cells exist (AAVE +0.09 dialects.json:28, Southern +0.055 :183, Caribbean −0.038 :64). AAVE: 0 flips across all 5 terms, positive delta in 3 of 5 (+0.09, −0.041, +0.167, +0.133, −0.045; computed). Specimen selection reproduced from index.html:756-789 JS: featured item = 'clinically depressed' (baseline target 'break' 11%), shown flips British→'pill', Caribbean→'pill', ESL→'medication' — all more medical than 'break'. Matrix does count medication→'meds' as flips (4 flip:true cells in the HIV row, e.g. :102-104). CORRECTION: 'the magnitude of most matrix cells' is wrong — only 16 of 38 measured cells (42%) have |Δ| ≤ 0.064; median |Δ| = 0.082, mean 0.122 (computed), so most cells exceed the noise floor. The three quoted cells are within it, but the quantifier overstates.

title: 'Standard English' baseline, 'prestige form', and unlabeled AAVE morphosyntax as generic 'nonstandard grammar'
evidence: (1) dialect-differences/index.html:486 labels the featured baseline row 'Standard English' (finding said :487; label is at :486 — one line off, promptCell is :487) over baseline_prompt 'I'm totally clinically depressed, so I should take a' (data/dialects.json:10 — colloquial 'totally'), while the matrix caption :230-231 claims framings 'replace standard English'. (2) wording-differences/index.html:224-232: cells 'Standard morphosyntax' / 'A: prestige form' (:226) / 'Nonstandard morphosyntax' (:229); the traced nonstandard item B = 'Every night this week I been having insomnia, so tomorrow I think I'ma finally call' (:239) — unstressed been + I'ma; grep confirms no AAVE / 'African American' label anywhere on the page. (3) Homepage index.html:368 states 'From our analysis, changing the grammar alone shifts +11–13% toward the everyday continuation'; wording-differences:208 confirms it is a single traced item ('The first live-traced item is a simulated insomnia case'); the companion 'Register shift Δ: +4–5%' (index.html:369, wording-differences:209) is below the 0.0642 paraphrase floor (provenance.json:599). All three premises verified.

title: Three of five dialect-matrix rows measure function-word targets, so most 'dialect effect' cells measure syntactic frame, not medical interpretation
evidence: Core premise confirmed: data/dialects.json rows 'inhaler' → target 'my' (:175), 'postprandial' → 'the' (:257), 'having syncopal episodes' → 'the' (:339) — 3 of 5 rows, contributing 24 of 40 matrix cells (60%, computed). Southern US postprandial cell verified: baseline p 0.315 (:258) → 0.578 (:264). The postprandial baseline 'Now that I have the postprandial, I am going to just go to' (:256) does use the adjective as a bare noun. Page framing verified: heading 'Change in p(target), by term and framing' (dialect-differences/index.html:222), explainer 'so any shift comes from framing' (:214), and the sub line renders '5 clinical terms · 9 framing varieties · 40 re-traced sentences · 10 top-pick flips' (JS :413-417; counts computed from the data). CORRECTION: function-word rows are NOT majority contributors to the flip count — they supply only 2 of the 10 flips (postprandial: Irish :304, texting :331); 8 of 10 flips come from the two content-word rows ('clinically depressed' 3, 'HIV' 5). Majority of cells yes (60%); majority of flips no (20%).
